settings.title: A Walled Garden Replanted With Proper Irrigation
settings.summary: We restored a neglected walled garden with deep borders and a drip system that survives dry spells.
settings.description:
An old walled garden behind a farmhouse near Cerne Abbas had good bones but exhausted soil and borders that fried every July. The walls gave it shelter and a sense of enclosure, but years of neglect had left the planting thin and the ground tired.

The problem

The owners loved the structure of the place and did not want to lose it. The trouble was that the south-facing borders heated up fast, the soil held almost no moisture, and anything planted there struggled to get through a hot stretch without constant hand watering.

Our approach

We started with the soil, digging in compost across the beds to rebuild structure and give the new planting something to hold on to. We then replanted with roses, hardy geraniums, and salvias, a combination chosen to suit a warm, walled position and to give a long run of flower through the season.

The piece that made the real difference was the irrigation. We ran a buried drip line on a timer so the borders get a slow soak at dawn, before the heat of the day, delivering water straight to the roots rather than losing it to evaporation off the surface.

  • Compost dug in across the beds to revive exhausted soil
  • Replanting with roses, hardy geraniums, and salvias
  • Buried drip line on a dawn timer for a slow, deep soak

The outcome

The planting held up through a hot, dry summer without daily watering, which was the test that mattered. The owners now spend far less time dragging a hose around, and the walled garden has gone back to being one of the best parts of the property.
